title: USA’s World Cup Group D rivals Turkey and Paraguay are battling injury worries
original_text:
With the World Cup rapidly approaching, major injury scares plague two of the United States’ Group D opponents .

While the Americans have had to grapple with some physical setbacks of their own, rivals Turkey and Paraguay in particular are hoping key players will be fully fit and in rhythm when the tournament kicks off in June.

Turkey stars Arda Güler (hamstring) and Hakan Çalhanoğlu (muscle strain) both look set to miss the remainder of the European club season, with Real Madrid and Inter Milan respectively. Though both are predicted to return in time to be available for the World Cup, the developments regarding both have been far from ideal for head coach Vincenzo Montella.

The Turkish Football Federation has not commented on the status of either, and is not expected to do so before it announces its World Cup squad at the end of May.

Güler, 21, had the most productive season in his young career with Madrid, delivering six goals and 14 assists across all competitions. What turned out to be the penultimate match of his season saw him produce a memorable brace against Bayern Munich in the Champions League.

Çalhanoğlu, the team’s captain, has missed a number of games this season with different muscle injuries. He is unlikely to feature in the Coppa Italia final in May, a game he helped drive Inter to reach with two goals and an assist in a decisive second-leg semifinal win over Como on April 21. The 32-year-old missed nine of Inter’s last 15 Serie A matches.

Both players are critical to Turkey, which will face the U.S. in Los Angeles on Group D’s third and final matchday on June 25, potentially with a spot in the knockout stage up for grabs. Çalhanoğlu has 104 caps, and the burgeoning Güler has 28. Both started all but one of their country’s World Cup qualifiers and are, without a doubt, key factors in any projected World Cup starting XI for their side.

Paraguay, too, has concerns before it takes on the U.S. in what will be the tournament opener for both teams on June 12, also in Southern California.

Diego Gomez, Brighton & Hove Albion’s 23-year-old midfielder, clutched his left leg at Tottenham two weeks ago after landing awkwardly and was substituted in his side’s 2-2 draw in the first half. He exited the pitch with his head in his shirt, fearing the worst.

Thankfully, Gomez avoided major injury. After 48 hours of doom-and-gloom speculation in Paraguay, Brighton manager Fabian Hurzeler calmed the waters and momentarily eased the stress of an entire nation.

“It’s not as bad as we expected,” Hurzeler said. “We have to go week to week. It’s not an injury that will take him out for months. It is something with his knee. He’s had a scan and we will have to rescan it in the next few days, but it is not as bad as some of us thought.”

It’s still an injury that will need mending. Gomez will likely miss the remainder of the Premier League season as he prioritizes his World Cup fitness. He’s not the only player nursing an issue.

Paraguay No. 10 Miguel Almiron has missed three MLS matches with what his club Atlanta United called “knee irritation.” Almiron, 32, is an emotional leader for his country and another attacking player critical to manager Gustavo Alfaro’s tactics.

Almiron was injured in Atlanta’s U.S. Open Cup win over Chattanooga FC on April 15 but is expected to rejoin first-team training this week, per a club spokesperson.

Back in England, Sunderland and Paraguay center back Omar Alderete limped off the pitch in the 63rd minute of his club’s 4-3 loss to Aston Villa. The 29-year-old Alderete is a prototypically rugged Paraguayan defender and another key piece for Alfaro. Sunderland head coach Régis Le Bris told reporters Alderete played ill against Villa.

“Last week we had a few players unwell, which can happen. So they were just on the edge, five of six of the players so it was massive for us,” Le Bris said. “But it is what it is. You have to manage the situation and for Omar it was just that point.”

The left-footed Alderete reappeared in Sunderland’s starting XI but struggled in defense in the club’s 5-0 loss to Nottingham Forest on April 24. It was an embarrassing defeat for Sunderland but Alderete’s inclusion meant Paraguay had averted another important injury. Still, last week ended with news that national team prospect and Portsmouth striker Gustavo Caballero, 24, is dealing with a groin injury. Caballero scored in Paraguay’s 2-1 loss to Morocco on March 31.

While Turkey and Paraguay have injury worries, Australia, the USMNT’s June 19 opponent in Seattle, is relatively healthy in terms of key players.

The most notable absence is Blackburn defender Lewis Miller, who suffered a torn achilles and will miss the World Cup. He had appeared in 16 straight matches for his country before the injury, including the loss to the USMNT in October.

Good news for the Socceroos came last weekend as center back Harry Souttar made his return from injury, making his first appearance in 16 months following a torn achilles. To boot, he scored for Leicester City.

Souttar, 27, has 36 caps for his country. He played every minute for Australia at the 2022 World Cup.

Norwich City forward Mohamed Touré had an injury scare this winter, but ultimately only missed a month of action. He has four goals in his last three matches for Norwich.
